questions.question: What is the main purpose of giving an AI coding agent a Temporal 'Skill'?

choices.text: To allow the AI agent to deploy Temporal workflows automatically on your behalf
To give the AI agent expert knowledge about Temporal concepts so it can help you write better Temporal code
To connect the AI agent directly to your Temporal Cloud account
To translate Temporal code from one programming language to another

questions.explanation: A Temporal Skill gives an AI coding agent domain-specific knowledge about how Temporal works — things like workflow rules, activity patterns, error handling, and more. Think of it like giving your AI assistant a Temporal textbook to study before helping you code. Without a Skill, the AI only knows what it learned during general training, which may be outdated or incomplete. With a Skill installed, it becomes a more reliable Temporal coding partner.

questions.question: Why does the Temporal Docs MCP Server give AI tools 'real-time access' to documentation rather than relying on their training data?

choices.text: Because AI tools cannot read documentation without special hardware
Because Temporal only allows AI tools to access docs through a paid subscription
Because training data can be outdated, and real-time access ensures the AI uses the most current and accurate Temporal documentation
Because the MCP Server translates documentation into a language the AI can understand

questions.explanation: AI models are trained on data up to a certain point in time, which means their knowledge can become outdated as software evolves. The Temporal Docs MCP Server solves this by connecting your AI assistant directly to Temporal's live documentation. This way, when you ask a question about Temporal, the AI draws from current docs rather than potentially stale training data — giving you more accurate and up-to-date answers.

questions.question: When getting started with Temporal for the first time, what does the quickstart guide recommend as your very first step?

choices.text: Deploy a Temporal Cloud account and configure authentication
Choose your programming language, install the matching SDK, and run a Hello World Workflow
Read all Temporal documentation before writing any code
Install all available Temporal SDKs to explore your options

questions.explanation: Temporal supports multiple programming languages, each with its own SDK (Software Development Kit — a set of tools and libraries for building with Temporal). The quickstart guide keeps things simple: pick the language you already know, install that language's SDK, and run a basic 'Hello World' Workflow. This hands-on approach lets you experience Temporal in action right away without needing to learn everything upfront.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the Temporal Developer Skill when added to an AI coding agent?

choices.text: It gives the AI agent direct access to your Temporal Cloud account to deploy workflows automatically
It provides the AI agent with expert-level knowledge of Temporal's programming model, including workflow determinism rules, activity patterns, retry policies, and common gotchas
It installs the Temporal SDK into your project and configures a local development server
It connects the AI agent to real-time Temporal documentation so it can answer questions about the latest API changes

questions.explanation: The Temporal Developer Skill gives AI coding agents domain-specific expertise about Temporal's programming model — things like determinism rules, activity patterns, retry policies, error handling, testing strategies, and common pitfalls. This is distinct from the Temporal Docs MCP Server, which provides real-time documentation access. Use the Developer Skill when you want your AI agent to write correct Temporal code without having to constantly correct it on fundamental patterns like not using non-deterministic code in workflows.

questions.question: How do you add the Temporal Developer Skill using the npx-based skills CLI, and which agents does this method support?

choices.text: Run `npx temporal install skill-developer`; supports only Claude Code
Run `npx skills add https://github.com/temporalio/skill-temporal-developer`; supports Claude Code, Codex, Cline, and other compatible agents
Run `npx skills install temporal-developer`; supports Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex only
Run `npx skills add temporal-developer@temporal-marketplace`; supports any agent that supports the MCP protocol

questions.explanation: The npx installation method uses `npx skills add <repository-url>` with the full GitHub URL of the skill. This approach works broadly across Claude Code, Codex, Cline, and other agents — not just a single tool. After installing via any method, you must restart your coding agent for the skill to take effect. This is a practical pattern to remember: install once, restart, and the agent gains Temporal expertise without any further configuration.

questions.question: What is a key difference between the Temporal Developer Skill and the Temporal Docs MCP Server?

choices.text: The Developer Skill works only with Claude Code, while the MCP Server works with any AI tool

The Developer Skill provides built-in Temporal expertise baked into the agent, while the MCP Server gives real-time access to current documentation so responses draw from up-to-date docs rather than training data
The MCP Server requires a paid Temporal Cloud subscription, while the Developer Skill is free to use
The Developer Skill handles Temporal Cloud connectivity issues, while the MCP Server handles local development workflow problems

questions.explanation: The Temporal Developer Skill embeds domain-specific Temporal knowledge (programming model, patterns, gotchas) directly into the agent's context, while the Temporal Docs MCP Server connects the agent to live documentation at query time. The MCP Server is particularly valuable because AI training data has a cutoff date — the MCP Server ensures responses reflect current API behavior and docs. In practice, you might use both: the Skill for coding assistance and the MCP Server for up-to-date reference information.

questions.question: When registering the Temporal Docs MCP Server with Claude Code, what flag would you add to make it available across ALL your projects rather than just the current one?

choices.text: --global
--all-projects
--scope user
--workspace shared

questions.explanation: The `--scope user` flag registers the MCP server globally so it is available in all your Claude Code projects. Without this flag, the configuration is stored in the project's `.mcp.json` file and only applies to that specific project. The full global command is: `claude mcp add --scope user --transport http temporal-docs https://temporal.mcp.kapa.ai`. This distinction matters in practice — if you work on multiple Temporal projects, registering globally saves you from having to configure the server in each one individually.

questions.question: Your team is using an AI coding agent to help build a new Temporal workflow. The agent suggests implementing a workflow that makes an HTTP call inside the workflow function to fetch configuration data on each execution. You know this is wrong, but you want the AI agent to understand WHY it's wrong and fix it properly. Which tool would give the AI agent the deepest understanding of Temporal's determinism constraints and activity patterns so it can self-correct this kind of mistake?

choices.text: Connect the AI agent to the Temporal Docs MCP Server so it can look up the HTTP client documentation at runtime

Install the Temporal Developer Skill, which gives the agent expert-level knowledge of workflow determinism rules and activity patterns, enabling it to recognize that side effects like HTTP calls must be wrapped in Activities
Install the Temporal Cloud Skill, which handles connectivity and authentication issues that might be causing the agent to suggest incorrect patterns
Point the agent to the Temporal quickstart for its language, since quickstarts demonstrate the correct Hello World pattern that avoids non-determinism

questions.explanation: The Temporal Developer Skill is specifically designed to give AI coding agents 'expert-level knowledge of Temporal's programming model — workflow determinism rules, activity patterns, retry policies, error handling, testing strategies, worker configuration, versioning, and common gotchas.' Making HTTP calls inside a Workflow function is a classic non-determinism violation: Workflows must be deterministic because Temporal replays workflow history to reconstruct state after failures. Any I/O operation — HTTP calls, database reads, random number generation — can produce different results on replay, causing the workflow to diverge from its history. These operations must be moved into Activities. The Temporal Developer Skill equips the agent to understand this constraint and proactively suggest the correct pattern (wrapping side effects in Activities) rather than just fixing the symptom. The Cloud Skill addresses connectivity issues, not programming model correctness. The MCP Server provides real-time doc lookup but doesn't embed deep Temporal expertise into the agent's reasoning. Quickstarts show basic patterns but don't convey the underlying determinism principles needed to self-correct in novel situations.

questions.question: Your team uses Claude Code as your primary AI coding assistant. You want it to have real-time access to current Temporal documentation rather than relying on potentially outdated training data. You run the MCP server registration command and then try to use it immediately, but the server isn't responding to Temporal documentation queries. What is the most likely reason?

choices.text: The MCP server only works with Claude Desktop, not Claude Code
You need to configure the server URL in the project's .mcp.json file before it will activate
You did not restart Claude Code and authenticate with your Google account after registration, both of which are required steps
The --scope user flag is incompatible with the HTTP transport and must be omitted

questions.explanation: The documentation explicitly states two required steps after running the registration command: (1) restart Claude Code, and (2) run '/mcp' to authenticate with your Google account. The MCP server requires anonymous authentication using a Google account to enforce rate limits and prevent abuse. Skipping either step means the server won't be active or won't be able to serve requests. This is a common gotcha when setting up MCP servers — engineers often assume that running the registration command is sufficient, but the restart-then-authenticate sequence is mandatory. The --scope user flag is valid and simply determines whether the config is stored globally or per-project (omitting it stores config in .mcp.json for project-scoped access). The MCP server works with both Claude Code and Claude Desktop, and any MCP-compatible tool. Understanding this setup pattern matters because authentication failures in developer tooling are often silent — the tool appears configured but doesn't actually work until the auth step completes.

questions.question: A platform engineer wants AI coding agents across the entire organization to have access to Temporal documentation, while also allowing individual project teams to optionally add project-specific MCP configurations. They configure the Temporal docs MCP server for Claude Code. Which approach correctly achieves the organization-wide availability requirement while preserving per-project flexibility?

choices.text: Run 'claude mcp add --transport http temporal-docs https://temporal.mcp.kapa.ai' without any scope flag in each project directory, which creates global config by default

Run 'claude mcp add --scope user --transport http temporal-docs https://temporal.mcp.kapa.ai' to store the config globally, making it available in all projects while teams can still add .mcp.json for project-specific servers
Add the server URL to each project's .mcp.json file and commit it to source control so all engineers get it automatically
Install the Temporal Developer Skill instead, since Skills are globally available and eliminate the need for per-project MCP configuration

questions.explanation: The --scope user flag stores the MCP server configuration at the user level, making it available across all projects without requiring any per-project setup. This is exactly what organization-wide availability requires. Teams can still add project-specific configurations via .mcp.json (which is what happens when you omit --scope user) for additional servers relevant to their project. Without the --scope user flag, the config goes into the project's .mcp.json file — which means every project would need to add it individually, and it only works for engineers who check out that specific project. Committing .mcp.json to source control (option C) solves distribution but couples the config to specific repositories and requires all engineers to use Claude Code. Skills (option D) provide domain expertise baked into the agent's reasoning, which is complementary to MCP servers but different in nature — Skills don't provide real-time documentation access. Understanding the distinction between user-scoped and project-scoped MCP configuration matters when designing developer tooling rollouts at scale.

questions.question: A team is building a complex Temporal application involving both workflow logic and Temporal Cloud deployment. They want their AI coding agent to help with both concerns. After installing only the Temporal Developer Skill, their agent struggles to help debug why their worker can't connect to Temporal Cloud despite correct-looking code. What does this reveal about the Skills architecture, and what should they do?

choices.text: The Temporal Developer Skill should handle Cloud connectivity since it covers 'worker configuration' — reinstalling it will fix the issue
The agent needs the Temporal Docs MCP Server instead of Skills, since MCP provides real-time access to Cloud troubleshooting guides

The two Skills are purpose-built for distinct problem domains: the Developer Skill covers programming model expertise while the Cloud Skill specifically addresses connectivity, authentication, and configuration issues — both should be installed for comprehensive coverage
Skills cannot coexist in the same agent session; they must choose either the Developer Skill or the Cloud Skill based on their primary use case

questions.explanation: Temporal provides two distinct Skills for different problem domains: the Temporal Developer Skill focuses on the programming model (determinism, activity patterns, retry policies, error handling, testing, versioning), while the Temporal Cloud Skill specifically addresses 'Temporal Cloud connectivity, authentication, and configuration issues.' These are separate concerns that require different expertise. Cloud connectivity failures often involve certificate authentication (mTLS), namespace configuration, endpoint URLs, network policies, and API keys — none of which are programming model concerns. A worker that has correct workflow code can still fail to connect due to misconfigured TLS certificates or wrong namespace addresses. Installing both Skills gives the agent comprehensive coverage: it understands HOW to write correct Temporal code (Developer Skill) AND how to properly connect and configure the Cloud environment (Cloud Skill). Skills are additive and can coexist — there's no conflict between them. The MCP Server complements Skills by providing real-time documentation access but doesn't replace the embedded expertise that Skills provide. This architectural separation reflects a sound engineering principle: specialized tools with clear boundaries outperform general-purpose tools trying to cover everything.

questions.question: Your team is building a complex order processing system using Temporal. A senior engineer proposes using an AI coding agent (with the Temporal Developer Skill installed) to generate workflow code that handles payment retries, inventory reservation via child workflows, and versioning for in-flight migrations. During code review, you notice the AI-generated workflow includes a call to a live inventory API directly inside the workflow function to 'check current stock levels before scheduling the child workflow.' The AI agent had access to the Temporal Docs MCP Server during generation. Which statement BEST explains why this pattern is dangerous in production, and what the MCP Server's real-time docs access should have caught?

choices.text: The issue is purely a latency concern — HTTP calls inside workflows are slow and will block the event loop, but the workflow will still be correct and eventually complete.

Direct I/O inside a workflow function violates determinism: on replay, the live API call will execute again and may return different data than the original execution, causing the workflow to take a different code path and potentially corrupting state or raising a non-deterministic error. The Temporal Developer Skill explicitly encodes this as a core gotcha — all non-deterministic operations must be wrapped in Activities.
The problem is that the Temporal MCP Server's rate limiting will throttle the workflow's API calls during high load, causing sporadic timeouts that are difficult to debug.
Child workflows cannot be scheduled after direct API calls due to an SDK ordering constraint; the workflow must complete all direct I/O before spawning any child workflows.

questions.explanation: Temporal workflows must be fully deterministic because the Temporal server replays workflow history to reconstruct state after worker restarts, crashes, or task queue rebalancing. During replay, the workflow code re-executes from the beginning, but instead of running activities again, it fast-forwards through recorded results. If you embed a live API call directly in the workflow function, it WILL execute again on every replay — and if the API returns different data (stock levels changed, network error, different timestamp), the workflow takes a different branch than the original execution recorded in history. This produces a non-deterministic workflow error, which Temporal will surface as a panic or exception, and the workflow will be stuck until manually reset. The fix is always to wrap any external I/O, randomness, or time-sensitive logic in an Activity, which is recorded in the event history and safely skipped during replay. This is one of the first principles the Temporal Developer Skill is designed to encode for AI agents — the skill covers 'workflow determinism rules' and 'common gotchas' explicitly. The MCP Server's real-time doc access should have pulled the determinism constraints from current Temporal documentation, but skill-based domain knowledge (not just doc retrieval) is what catches these subtle architectural anti-patterns before they reach production. The broader lesson: AI-assisted code generation for Temporal is only as safe as the domain knowledge embedded in the skill — always review AI-generated workflow code for hidden non-determinism, especially any function calls that touch external systems, clocks, or random values.

questions.question: A platform team is evaluating two approaches for giving their AI coding agents Temporal expertise. Approach 1: Install the Temporal Developer Skill via 'npx skills add' and rely on it for workflow authoring guidance. Approach 2: Configure only the Temporal Docs MCP Server (https://temporal.mcp.kapa.ai) and rely on real-time documentation retrieval for all Temporal questions. During a production incident, the team needs their AI agent to correctly advise on whether a long-running workflow (running for 8 months) that used GetVersion() for a code migration can safely call continue-as-new without losing versioning markers. Which approach is MORE likely to give correct, actionable guidance, and why?

choices.text: Approach 2 (MCP Server only) is superior because it retrieves current documentation in real-time, avoiding any stale knowledge baked into the Skill's training data, and Temporal's versioning + continue-as-new interaction is thoroughly documented.

Approach 1 (Developer Skill only) is superior because Skills encode deep programming model expertise — including subtle cross-feature interactions like versioning markers and continue-as-new — that cannot be reliably derived from documentation retrieval alone, especially under incident pressure.

Both approaches are equivalent for this scenario because the Temporal Developer Skill is simply a curated index of the same documentation the MCP Server retrieves, so the output will converge to the same answer.
Neither approach can help; versioning and continue-as-new interactions require reading the workflow's actual event history in Temporal Cloud, which neither the Skill nor the MCP Server has access to.

questions.explanation: This question exposes a critical architectural distinction between two complementary but fundamentally different AI augmentation mechanisms. The Temporal Docs MCP Server provides real-time retrieval of documentation — it is excellent for factual lookups ('what is the default schedule-to-close timeout?') and keeping the agent current with API changes. However, documentation rarely encodes the subtle emergent behaviors that arise from feature interactions in production. The interaction between GetVersion() (used for safe in-place code migration) and continue-as-new is a canonical example: when a workflow calls continue-as-new, it starts a NEW workflow execution with a fresh event history. Versioning markers created by GetVersion() in the OLD execution do NOT carry over to the new execution. If the new execution's code paths depend on those version markers to avoid deprecated branches, and the markers are absent, the workflow may execute the wrong (possibly removed) code path — or worse, behave correctly in testing but fail on workers that have already removed the old code branch. This is precisely the kind of cross-feature gotcha ('versioning' + 'continue-as-new' + 'worker deployment sequencing') that the Temporal Developer Skill is explicitly designed to encode as domain expertise. The skill's knowledge of 'versioning' and 'common gotchas' represents synthesized engineering judgment, not just documentation retrieval. In practice, the best production setup uses BOTH: the Skill for deep programming model reasoning and the MCP Server for current API references. But under incident pressure, when the question is 'will this break?', domain-encoded expertise in the Skill is more reliably actionable than hoping the documentation covers the exact cross-feature edge case you're facing. The takeaway: treat Skills and MCP Servers as complementary layers — retrieval for facts, Skills for judgment.
